description.task: Implement a greedy coin change algorithm that works on canonical coin systems (like US coins: 1, 5, 10, 25 cents). At each step, pick the largest coin that fits. Count how many coins are used and list them.
instructions: Greedy works optimally for canonical coin systems but may fail for arbitrary denominations (e.g., {1,3,4}, amount=6)
description.expectedOutput:
41 cents: [25, 10, 5, 1] (4 coins)
30 cents: [25, 5] (2 coins)
11 cents: [10, 1] (2 coins)

description.task: Demonstrate when greedy fails by solving coin change for non-canonical coin sets with both greedy and DP. Show that greedy can choose suboptimally while DP always finds the minimum.
instructions: All implementations are provided — this is a read-and-run demonstration exercise
Non-canonical {1,3,4}: greedy picks 4, then 1+1 = 3 coins; DP finds 3+3 = 2 coins
The greedy choice property only guarantees optimality for canonical coin systems
Understand why: greedy commits to the first choice without reconsidering
description.hint: This is a read-and-run exercise. All code is implemented. Focus on understanding when greedy produces a suboptimal solution vs the globally optimal DP solution.
description.expectedOutput:
Coins {1,3,4} amount=6:
  Greedy: 3 coins
  DP:     2 coins
Coins {1,5,10,25} amount=30:
  Greedy: 2 coins
  DP:     2 coins

description.task: Given an array where each element is the maximum jump length from that position, determine if you can reach the last index from index 0. Use a greedy approach: track the farthest index reachable at each step.
instructions: [3,2,1,0,4]: index 3 has nums[3]=0 and is the max reach → stuck, false
[2,3,1,1,4] min jumps: from 0 jump to index 1(val=3), then jump to end → 2 jumps
description.expectedOutput:
[2,3,1,1,4] can jump: true
[3,2,1,0,4] can jump: false
[2,3,1,1,4] min jumps: 2
[2,3,0,1,4] min jumps: 2
